notes: Assertions around import paths and emoji usage pass 100% in both configurations — these are universal conventions that don't require a skill

Eval 2 (update-middleware-table) assertion was poorly designed — request_id already existed in the table. With-skill improved the entry description; without-skill did nothing. The skill drove a better outcome not reflected by the pass rate
Eval 3 with-skill had a duplicate section bug (inserted 'Using the Cache' twice) that the assertions don't catch — the skill didn't prevent this quality issue
No timing/token data available — timing.json was not captured from subagent notifications

Both configurations handled eval 1 (creating CACHING.md) equally well because caching documentation follows well-known patterns. The skill's value is more evident for project-specific conventions like matching config keys and doc format conventions
